text.title: Rust Error Handling Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules covering library/app split, propagation, and context preservation.

rule.statement: Publishable libraries use `thiserror` to derive `Error` on an enum of named variants. Each variant has `#[error("...")]` for Display and optional `#[from]` for source-chain conversion.
rule.rationale: Libraries must let downstream consumers `match` on specific variants. Opaque dynamic errors force callers to log-only, losing flow control.

rule.statement: Binaries (CLI, web service, daemon) use `anyhow::Result<T>` with `.context()` calls at every fallible boundary. No custom Error enums unless a specific call site needs to match.
rule.rationale: App-level code log-and-exits on most errors. Manual enum boilerplate adds no value when the caller is the user-facing log. `.context()` builds the trace from existing strings.

rule.statement: `.unwrap()` and `.expect()` are FORBIDDEN in `src/` (non-test code). Allowed in `#[cfg(test)]` blocks and `tests/`. Enforce via clippy `unwrap_used = "deny"`, `expect_used = "deny"` in `clippy.toml`.
rule.rationale: Panics in library code crash callers without explanation. `?` and proper error variants preserve context and let callers decide.

rule.statement: Use `?` operator for propagation. Explicit `match result { Err(e) => return Err(e.into()), Ok(v) => v }` is forbidden unless the match adds genuine logic (logging, fallback, retry).
rule.rationale: Manual match shadows the `From` conversion and bloats code. `?` triggers `From` automatically — chain `thiserror` `#[from]` and propagation is one character.

rule.statement: Every error variant that wraps a lower-level error MUST expose it via `#[source]` or `#[from]`. Never `.to_string()` the source — that drops the chain.
rule.rationale: Source chains let observability tools (`tracing`, Sentry) walk causes. Stringifying the source erases the chain and the original type.

rule.statement: When using `anyhow`, call `.context("doing X")` on every `?` boundary that touches I/O, network, or external input. Plain `?` without context drops the "where did this fail" trace.
rule.rationale: Anyhow errors print backwards-chained context — without `.context()` calls the chain is empty and the user sees only the lowest-level error (e.g. "permission denied" with no file name).
